speaker: interviewer
text: --with Morris Kozak. The date is August the 9th, 1996. Morris, you just finished telling us what happened when you were working in the field.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. I was working there, I will say, a few weeks. Then I was plowing in a different place there, a lot of-- maybe a couple of weeks later. I was plowing in a different spot, also for my-- for my boss. And adjacent piece of land was a Ukrainian plowing too. And he comes over and he says-- he hollers to me, hey, he says, come over. Let's have a smoke. I said sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: I stopped the horses. He comes over. He starts talking to me. And we smoke his cigarettes. Then who-- who are you? Where are you from? I say, I'm-- I'm a prisoner of war. There was a lot of war prisoners working on different farms, different places. I don't know if he detected anything or what about my looks. I spoke fluent Ukrainian. And he says, and he-- he report it. And that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later the evening, I bring home the horses. My boss comes over to me. He says, [? Fyodor, ?] he says, you'll have to run away from here. He says, you was talking to somebody while you was plowing. I said, yeah. He says, he came to me and told me that you're a Jew. And if you-- and if I won't chase you out, he's going to bring the Ukrainian police to get rid of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I used to sleep with the horses, you know, they had in the stable. But that night, I didn't sleep at all. I had like a-- a deck, you know? I used to sleep there. And that night, I didn't sleep already in-- in the stable. And whatever I had, two, three rags, a shirt, a pair of pants, I had it with me. And he told me to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept in back of the barn. In case they come, I can run in the fields. I'll see them drive in. Anyway, nobody came that night. In the morning, I went out on the highway and I figure out-- I was going to go to his father, the one I was working before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] as I start walking on the highway, a lot of Ukrainians, they were taking that tax wheat. They used to pay their taxes with wheat or so to a different town. And I hollered to one-- to one, how about giving me a lift? He says, come on. As soon as I got on the wagon, within five minutes, the topic came up, the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says to me, thank God, he says, we got rid of all these Jews here. Now, we're going to have a clean Ukraine. Oh, I say, thank God. I said, it's the best thing ever happened. And the whole conversation was Jews, and Jews, and Jews, what they did, and what they doing, and it's the best thing we're going to have a clean country. Anyway, I-- he gave me a lift, I'll say, about 15 miles, 15 kilometers. Then he wanted to take me-- I told him who I am. I told him I'm a Czech. I'm a grandson of a so-and-so. So he knew, you knew, the boss I was working for, he was well known. He was a very well-to-do man. I said I'm his grandson, just in case he detects a dialect or something. I'm not 100%. Anyway, he took out-- he gave me a-- a pocketful of tobacco. He says-- and gave me a pat on the back. He says, you're one of my sons. He says, you're a [INAUDIBLE]. Thank God you believe that way. Then he want to take me to the main highway. I say, no. I knew my way around there. Every-- I knew every-- every stone there. And I got off in a village called Porlic. It was a-- you know, a well-known town. And I start--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Porlic, P-O-R-L-I-C, I imagine. Maybe T. You know, in Polish, we used to write it different. Anyway, and I had to walk from that spot to go to his father, the one I was working before, Prokop. His name was Prokop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-R, Pro, O-K-O-P, Prokop. And-- and I'm walking. It was, you know, like a dirt road. And I came-- that dirt road, you had to cross a main highway. You used to-- as I had to-- before I crossed it, I hear a wagon down the road. But I figure, as I came before I cross it there was a-- a house built a catty-corner. And it was a-- a picket fence there, and there was a lot of growths.

speaker: interviewer
text: I figure, and I hear noises, you know, as they're coming closer. And I lay down right under there before I-- sure enough, there was a wagon full or Ukrainian police driving. As they drove by in-- as soon as I didn't hear the noise from the wagon, it was a cobblestone road, you know? You hear the wagon quite a bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as I didn't hear a wagon, I run across the road and through the back. I went in. There were gardens and the barns. In the back of the barns, they had gardens, big gardens. Got in through the garden. I crawled in because I knew my way around there. I was working there before. I crawled into the barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm peeking out between the-- between the boards, you know? And I see the help, the farmhands there. And then I see the-- Mrs. Prokop. [INAUDIBLE] and I called her, [INAUDIBLE] [? Prokova, ?] [INAUDIBLE] [? Prokova. ?] All of a sudden, she comes in. She crossed her heart and says, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews were already liquidated in Mlynow. I'm telling you, the Jews were already killed out. And the Ukrainians were hunting and the Germans hunting for Jews, of any left-overs. They were catching them and killing them. Anyway, she says, crawl into the straw. Don't tell nobody that you are here. Sure enough, I crawled in. An hour later, she brought me some bread and other stuff. And they kept me for three days. And they brought me food a couple of times a day. Then he came in on the third day, Mr. Prokop. And he knew the situation. He says, my father sent me a note from the ghetto with that Prokop's son, which lived in-- in Mlynow. And he handed over that little note to Prokop. And he gave me the note where he was going to be in hiding. Anyway, I-- on the third day he came in. He says, tomorrow morning, he says, 4 o'clock, he says, we're going to go. He says, I'm going to Targowica, to a different town there. And he's driving by Morawica and he's going to-- he gives me the-- the-- the reins. I should drive the horses in a big coat on me. And he's sitting next to me in a wagon. As we're driving, he says to me, in case we're stopped, you don't say a word. I'll do all the talking. Everybody knew him, very respectful man. Sure enough, nobody stopped us. We came to that town, Morawica, which turn. I jumped off. And he says to me, [SPEAKING POLISH] which means God be with you. Sure enough, I walked up to the house where the-- my father was hiding, and my mother, and everybody was hiding there, called Anopro Cherachuk. He was an old friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anopro, Anopro. His second name was Cherachuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anopro was-- is A-N-O-P-R-O. Cherchuk is C-H-E-R-C-H-U-K, Cherchuk. And this was early in the morning. See, we left-- it wasn't 4:00 because we left maybe 3 o'clock in the morning. It was still dark. As I walked out there, I knocked to the door of Anopro, and he seen me. Oh my God, he says, [? Moshko. ?] They used to call me in Ukrainian, [? Moshko. ?] Anyway, he took me right into the barn where my whole family was. My father had snuck everybody out from the ghetto. This is a different story altogether. I wasn't there. But my father has been telling me how he got everybody out, what I made the arrangement with him. You-- were you-- we were only supposed to come for two weeks to stay with him. But he thought after two weeks, after everybody is killed out, you know, it'll be all over. Then we were free. But it wasn't so. And we were on top of the straw. They pulled me up, and we all together again. And they were there already for a week. They heard every shot because the killing from Mylnow took place only maybe a kilometer, a kilometer and a half. And they heard all the-- all the shooting going on. They killed out all the Jews. Anyway, later on, we were there. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. I wanted to ask you, how did your father know to come to this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It turned out we were friends-- friends for many, many years. Our grandfathers were friends. You know, this was families we lived together. I mean, the Ukrainians at one time, all that was discrimination a little bit, you know, but we lived together. It was a way of life. And they used to come to my father sometimes. He needed couples a-- couple dollars, you know, to lend him. Anyway, so by-- by chance, he went to him. We knew a lot of people. And we were there, though on top of the straw was no good. So in the-- in the barn-- the barn was built from boards all around. And we took a corner in the barn inside the straw and we made a hole, I would say, 4 feet by 6 feet, 4 maybe 6 and 1/2 feet, made a hole. You couldn't stand up. You had to always be on your knees. And we were in the straw. Adjacent to the barn there was a pigsty. Used to keep--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this hole for that you dug?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we lay in there. We shouldn't be on top of the straw. We wanted to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: You lay in this-- your whole family lay in this hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family, we got in there. We made a passage alongside a wall to crawl out whenever we have to. And we used to pull in a bundle of straw from the end. If anybody looked at it, it wasn't a hole. In other words, we pulled it in as we walked in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in this hole during the day and night?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you couldn't stand up in this hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- no. We couldn't stand up. There are two windows was in the two boards had two little knots knocked out of the boards. You know the knot? And one knot, one window, this was our windows. One was facing the yard in the front. And the other was facing the side. So more, 95% of the time somebody was standing and looking out of that window, out of that knot to see what goes-- is going on. Inside the barn in the middle, you could drive through. And if anybody got a little dangerous or something, Anopro had a cough. He used to go [COUGHS]. He used to start coughing. And we-- we used to-- we used to whisper anyway. We always whispered. We never spoke loud in case somebody walks by. But when he coughed, we knew a sign. We have to be completely silent. Some neighbor came over or somebody came over. We had-- he used to cough. That was a way. Anyway, we were laying there. He was a poor man himself. He had a son living with him, His wife. Her name was Khrystia. And he had a son, Aleksei, and a wife. Aleksei had a wife and a daughter. He had a-- the wife was Anka and a daughter, [? Vera. ?] They all knew about us. And the son didn't bother with us too much. We knew it, but he was one of the younger ones. He already belong to the-- to the Ukrainian-- they called themselves-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hm-- it'll come to me. He belonged to the Ukrainian Nationalist parties and stuff like that, which they were definitely again Jews. That was a no-no. A Jew didn't have to [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was the-- the head of the organization. And he used to go to the meetings there. And we were laying there, and then winter came around. We had our-- our--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what year was this? The winter of--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the end of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: October was the killing of all the Jews. And my father probably came in there maybe a week or 10 days before. And I'm mixing up different stories. Anyway, when-- when he-- I was told when he had smuggled everybody out of that place and he tied up the horses to a tree, they find the horses without my father. And he was driving for that Ukrainian. He was the main guy from all around. His name was Savitskii. He went into the ghetto with Ukrainian police and Germans. They were digging up the whole ghetto looking for my father, whatever happened to him. Anyway, they couldn't find him. But this is a-- this, I wasn't there at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Winter setted in and we had any straw, and winter was terrible, cold, very cold. And we had one-- not a blanket. What do you call it? A-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anyway, it'll come to me. This was the only thing we were covered with. In other words, we used to cover ourselves. Put it in the land. And that's how we covered ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came very filthy, dirty. We didn't have a way of washing ourselves. We didn't have nothing. We had tremendous-- we-- me my brother got diseased between our fingers. And we-- we had different boils all over our legs. And, you know, not the top body, the bottom in the creases where your arms start the fingers from filth. And we had lice, big as-- as I don't know what. And this was in the wintertime. Our toilet was a bucket. We had a bucket with a piece of rag. We used to go on that-- on that bucket at night. Most of the time, I used to do it, crawl through that hole and pull out a bucket. Dump it, we use to keep the manure from the horses and the cows. That was the place. And this was-- this was the way it done. They used to send us in a little bit of water, a few potatoes, whatever they had. They had very little. And-- and it got so bad from the disease we had that my pants from the pus was stiff. They soaked in with pus. I have no hair on my legs, not one hair. It's all new skin. And it got real bad. But we didn't want them to know inside that we are diseased because if they'll find out that we have a disease, you might want to chase us out. So they never knew it. One night, my sister-- we had a-- a Polish family there-- fine people, because they was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish family was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right not in between, but a kilometer away, maybe a kilometer and a quarter. He was living on the main highway there. His name was Federovich. He was not a doctor. He was like a-- we used to call him a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] used to do little things, you know? Anyway, my father went over there to him. He says, look, and even when we seen him, we didn't know what to-- you know, like we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --took him out from a grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. So your father left the hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hiding, and he went over there to him. My father was diseased a little bit too. Was it? And he told him, he says, I'm going to make you up a-- a sap-- a-- from sulphur and fit-- pig, pig fat, mix it all up. You come tomorrow, he says, I'll have it ready for him. We didn't trust too many people. So my sister went over there. My sister Jean went over there three days later at night, in evening. Everything was on at night, not during the day. Nothing was-- and sure enough, he had a-- a pot full of that stuff. He brought back, we put that stuff on. And this spring start to come already. We put it on like somebody peeled her a banana. The stuff disappeared. Then we figure at night, we're going to take a bath. We took like a big-- from wood, you know, they used to-- they wash clothes in it. We got some water in the middle of the-- of that barn. Took a bath, and that was a big mistake. The disease came right back. We didn't have no clean clothes and stuff like that. Anyway, put on that stuff again. We didn't wash ourselves no more. Didn't get washed for God knows how long. And it helped us. And they never find out that we were diseased. And that went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, he didn't have enough to give us to eat. My sister used to go out. That was daytime, used to sneak out, put on a scarf, look like the peasants, you know? We knew certain people we could go to, pick up couple of breads, a bread, or whatever. And sure enough, and I used to go out once in a while to [INAUDIBLE], certain people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- one time we didn't have no-- didn't have enough food in the house. We-- we brought them in from a different farm. We stole that, brought it into, and they never knew. Never-- he never asked where it came from. In the wintertime, in the spring already, didn't have enough to eat. A little further up there was a little creek going there. We went over the creek. The farmers used to cover the potatoes for the winner, take a pile of potatoes, cover it with straw and earth. It shouldn't freeze. And in the spring, they used to take it up. Sure enough, I dug in a hole-- at night we used to work it-- dug into a hole to them potatoes, we used to bring potatoes. He never ask where it came from. They ate. We ate. In the spring, the farmer didn't find a potato. It was all empty. Probably, he thought the [INAUDIBLE], the Jews took it away. We knew who it was, too. We knew the farmer. So this was a way of life. Every day was something, every day. He was a very religious man. He used to go to church on Sundays. He'd say, used to say, I prayed for you. And one time he came. He talking to the hall. He says, they were talking, he says. They seen us with machine guns walking around. He was laughing. All, you know, the different farmers. But he knew better. Anyway, what I'm trying to bring out, it was very hard. We lay there whole summer. Summer wasn't too bad because it was warm. We were laying that hole. But we got so used to it, just like you put somebody in a-- something in a box. he lays there, and that's how it was. The main entrance was they used to bring us the food through the-- through the pigsty. The granddaughter, most of the time, used to have two buckets. She had a little bit of potatoes, a little bit of water, whatever they had. In the pigsty, a board was cut against a-- a post. But it was cut inside where we were laying, was cut. Used to push away that board and used to hand us in the-- hand us in that pot. They hardly ever saw us. This-- tell you an incident one time. Two Ukrainian police drive up on a-- on the-- on the-- on the farm, on the-- on the yard, drive up with horse and wagon and with their-- with their guns and everything. And but the old man is in there in the barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we could see through the window who they came in. And the old man is already coughing, giving us a sign. But we already knew. And wants us to don't open. Do you know of any Jews around anywhere? This was later on in the summertime. He says, what the hell do I need with Jews for, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he says to him, if you hear of any Jews, he says, let us know, he says. We'll take care of them. Oh, he says, [INAUDIBLE]. I certainly will, the old man. We hear everything through the straw while they're talking. One policeman decides-- excuse my expression-- he has to urinate. He goes into the pigsty. And he stands against that pole, over that board where they used to give us the food. And he urinates right on that board. And we sitting here. And he's sitting there. That's how close life was, you know, death. And sure enough, he said goodbye to him. And if you hear anything, let me know. That was the conversation. He went away. That's how close. That's how life was. If he would only know we-- we were sitting on the other side of the board. So every day, was something, every day. This went on for 18-- 18 months, 18 and 1/2 months we sat there. And once in a while, they bring us a Ukrainian paper, hand us in. And we could read through the-- through the lines that the front, they shorten the front. They shorten. So we knew that it's going to come to an end. Sooner, sure enough, that son went to a meeting one time. And they were discussed-- they want to-- they want to make a raid on our-- on this father's farm. See, there's-- there's rumors that his father's holding Jews. There's rumors. He came back. Although he wasn't-- he never saw us, even, his son. Only the granddaughter used to bother with us and the old people. And he told his father, you better get rid of those Jews, he says, because they're going to come and raid your place. Anopro comes into the pigsty. He says, save yourself. Save yourself. It's bad. My son came, and he told me to-- to tell yous to get. Sure enough, I don't remember. The Polacks and the Ukrainians were fighting each other. The Ukrainians used to burn Polish farms. Then there was a Polack there. And the Polacks used to fight-- because we had a lot of Polacks living on-- Polish people. And my father had a friend. His name was Jaska Olfans. He had a farm, I'll say, about kilometer and a half away from we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish, this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, was Polish. He grew up with my father. They were boyfriends. They grew up together. We going back from generations. I don't know if my sister went there or my father went there, I can't recall, when-- when we heard the news. And the Polish farmers, there was a town [POLISH]. We weren't far from [POLISH]. They used to leave the farms alone overnight. And they used to go to sleep in town where it was safer because the Ukrainians used to burn down their farms. And my father went over to Jaska, or my sister. I can't recall, exactly. He says, I'm in trouble. I need a place. Jaska says-- he says, with the family. Jaska says, you come here. But you're on own your own risk because I'm not here at night. If they burn you down-- if they burn me down, they burn me down. He had horses and, you know, cows and everything. Sure enough, that night was the middle of winter. This was on second winter already. It was very, very cold. The snow must have been 4, 5 feet high. And we knew our way, you know, how to-- all of a sudden, we hear shots. It was-- the moon was out, you know, and the snow was bright. We hear pow, pow, shooting at us. We fell into the snow, all of us. We lay there for about 5, 10 minutes. We start to crawl away, crawl away. And finally, we-- we got on the other side. And we got into Jaska's. Nobody there, so we crawled into the barn. It was snowing that night and covered all the tracks, couldn't see no tracks or nothing. We were always afraid of tracks from the snow. Fresh snow, if you walk, you shouldn't leave no track. We crawled into there. In the morning Jaska came in. Didn't know. And she was very-- make a pot of potatoes and all kind-- they didn't know what to do for us. And he says to us, look, he says. It's not safe where you're at. And it's not safe in the house. He had a cellar, but a cellar goes into the ground on steps. He had a lot of potatoes there and stuff. He says at night, you sleep in the cellar. And sure enough, then we start. As we were laying, we could hear the-- the cannons there, the fire, the earth trembling from the-- from the shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris, we're going to take a break here again, and then we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come back.
